signature: Exponential Diophantine finiteness: orbits/recursive sequences hitting exponentially thin sets EXACTLY (perfect powers, S-units, exact boundaries), with multiplicative independence of 2 and 3 as the rigidity source. Sub-smells hunted: many-term S-unit equations shaped like the Collatz cycle equation n(2^K − 3^L) = Σ 3^{L−1−i}2^{a_i}; mod-m local-global certificates for exponential predicates (= our regular-invariant-set certificates); point-level two-base digit rigidity (Stewart/Senge–Straus); gcd non-conspiracy of 2-power and 3-power families.

finds.ref: Prajeet Bajpai, Michael A. Bennett — Effective S-unit Equations Beyond 3 Terms: Newman's Conjecture — 2023, arXiv:2308.05162 (with precursor: Bajpai–Bennett, Sums of two S-units via Frey–Hellegouarch curves, 2016, arXiv:1603.07922)
finds.the_theorem: Theorem 1: Let K be a number field, S a finite set of places containing all infinite places with |S| ≤ 3, and a_1,...,a_5 fixed nonzero elements of K. Then there is an effectively computable upper bound on the heights of nondegenerate solutions (degenerate = some two-term subsum a_iu_i + a_ju_j = 0) of a_1u_1 + ... + a_5u_5 = 0 in S-units u_j. Theorem 6 (uniform version): same conclusion when the coefficients are not fixed but satisfy H(a_i) ≤ κ1·h(u)^κ2, with bound depending only on K, S, κ1, κ2. Application: explicit resolution of Newman's question (Erdős–Graham p.80) on ω(N) = #representations N = 2^a3^b + 2^c + 3^d; cf. Evertse–Győry–Stewart–Tijdeman: ω(N) ≤ 4 for N > N_0 (ineffective). Methods: Baker + p-adic linear forms in two logarithms, a 'matching procedure' for 5 terms; the 2016 precursor does 2-term-plus-power cases via Frey–Hellegouarch curves and modularity.
finds.the_match: Over Q, |S| ≤ 3 means S = {∞, 2, 3}: EXACTLY the {2,3}-unit world. The cycle equation with L odd steps, K halvings is n·2^K − n·3^L − Σ_{i=0}^{L−1} 3^{L−1−i}2^{a_i} = 0: an (L+2)-term S-unit equation with coefficient vector (n, −n, −1,...,−1) (3-powers absorbed into the units). For L = 3 this is 5 terms. The unknown coefficient n is handled by Theorem 6: Rhin-type lower bounds on |2^K − 3^L| (linear forms in two logs of 2 and 3) give n ≤ L·3^L·K^{13.3}·O(1) ≤ κ1·h(u)^2, verifying the κ-hypotheses. Nondegeneracy is automatic by sign/parity analysis (n odd, a_i < K kills all two-term cancellations). GAPS, honestly: (a) 3-odd-step cycles are already excluded by cycle-specific m-cycle work (Simons–de Weger/Hercher to 91 blocks), so the L=3 content is methodological — a new proof from general-purpose machinery; (b) the interesting L ≥ 4 needs effective 6+-term S-unit equations, open even for |S| = 3; (c) their method ignores our extra structure (monotone exponents a_0 < ... < a_{L−1} < K, fixed coefficient pattern), which is unexploited slack in our favor. Same template fits the Antihydra prefix identity 2^k·a_k = 3^k·a_0 − Σ 3^{k−1−i}2^i·e_i (e_i ∈ {0,1}): few-odd-steps prefixes are sparse S-unit relations.
finds.transfer: Concrete statement to prove: 'Every Collatz cycle with at most 3 odd steps has minimum element below an explicit effective bound, uniformly, via 5-term S-unit machinery' — then extend to L = 4 (6 terms) by pushing their matching procedure using monotone-exponent structure: publishable as 'effective 6-term S-unit equations with ordered exponents, with application to cycles of rational transductions'.

finds.ref: J.-H. Evertse, H.P. Schlickewei, W.M. Schmidt — Linear equations in variables which lie in a multiplicative group — 2002, Annals of Mathematics 155(3), 807–836
finds.the_theorem: Theorem 1.1 (verified from full text): K a field of characteristic 0, Γ ≤ (K*)^n a subgroup of finite rank r, a_1,...,a_n ∈ K*. The number A(a_1,...,a_n;Γ) of nondegenerate solutions x ∈ Γ of a_1x_1 + ... + a_nx_n = 1 (nondegenerate: no nonempty proper subsum vanishes) satisfies A ≤ exp((6n)^{3n}(r+1)). Uniform in the coefficients a_i; via quantitative Subspace Theorem. Ineffective for heights (counts solutions only).
finds.the_match: Normalize the cycle equation by n·2^K: 1 = (3^L/2^K) + Σ_i (1/n)·3^{L−1−i}·2^{a_i−K}, variables in the rank-2 group generated coordinatewise by 2, 3, coefficients (1, 1/n,...,1/n). For each FIXED n and L: at most exp((6(L+2))^{3(L+2)}·3-ish) parity-vector shapes close a cycle on n — a bound uniform in the SIZE of n and in K. GAPS, honestly: (a) the coefficient vector varies with n, so absorbing it costs rank+1 per n; the count is per-n, NOT uniform over all n simultaneously — and since each parity vector determines its n uniquely, per-n counting does not yield global finiteness (this is exactly where the conjecture lives); (b) the bound is superexponential in L, quantitatively vacuous for small K (trivial counting binom(K,L) is finite anyway once K is bounded) — the genuine content is K-uniformity; (c) degenerate solutions need separate treatment (mostly excluded by positivity). This is the precise answer to 'what do ESS bounds say about parity-vector weights': finiteness of weight-L cycle-admissible parity vectors per starting value, uniform in K and in the size of n.
finds.transfer: Formulate and prove: 'for each L, each odd n lies on at most C(L) = exp((6(L+2))^{3(L+2)}·4) cycles with L odd steps, uniformly in n and K'; then measure the true count numerically for L ≤ 10 to see how absurdly far ESS is from truth (likely 0 or 1) — the gap is a target for exploiting monotone-exponent structure in the subspace framework.

finds.ref: Michael A. Bennett — On Some Exponential Equations of S. S. Pillai — 2001, Canadian Journal of Mathematics 53(5), 897–922
finds.the_theorem: Theorem 1.1 (verified from full text): If a, b, c are nonzero integers with a, b ≥ 2, then a^x − b^y = c has at most TWO solutions in positive integers (x, y). Sharp (infinite family with exactly two). Conjecture 1.2: at most one solution outside an explicit finite list. Tools: lower bounds for linear forms in logarithms of two algebraic numbers plus elementary gap arguments. Related later work: Scott–Styer and the generalized Pillai equation ±ra^x ± sb^y = c; Chim–Pink–Ziegler: Pillai's problem U_n − V_m = c between two linear recurrences with mult. independent dominant roots has effectively finitely many solutions.
finds.the_match: Two instantiations. (i) The family 2^K − 3^L = c: cycle analysis lives on small values of |2^K − 3^L| (it divides Σ); Bennett gives the two-solution rigidity for every fixed c uniformly — the count structure behind the classical Steiner/Simons–de Weger use of two-log linear forms. (ii) BMO 5 cryptid (f(x) = 10·2^x − 1; b grows ×3 between staircase hits; halts iff b = f(a) − 1 exactly): IF the b-orbit had exact exponential epochs b_t = d·3^t + e, halting instances would embed in the generalized Pillai family r·3^t − s·2^a = c with at-most-two/finiteness structure. GAP, and it is the essential one: cryptid orbits are floor-perturbed, not exact exponentials — O(1) corrections accumulate per step (the parity digits), which is precisely where the hardness lives. Pillai technology covers only the zero-entropy shadow of the dynamics.
finds.transfer: Catalog which cryptid halting predicates become generalized-Pillai after epoch subsampling (exact closed form on a subsequence): for BMO 5, compute whether resets admit eventually-exact epochs; each such case is immediately finite-decidable by Bennett/Scott–Styer plus explicit two-log bounds (Laurent–Mignotte–Nesterenko constants).

finds.ref: C. L. Stewart — On the representation of an integer in two different bases — 1980, J. reine angew. Math. (Crelle) 319, 63–72
finds.the_theorem: For multiplicatively independent integers a, b ≥ 2 there is an effectively computable C = C(a,b) such that for all n > 25: s_a(n) + s_b(n) > log log n / (log log log n + C) − 1, where s_a(n) = number of nonzero digits of n base a. (Effectivization, via Baker's linear forms in logarithms plus Loxton–van der Poorten multiplicative relations, of Senge–Straus 1973: only finitely many integers have bounded digit sums in two multiplicatively independent bases.) Statement cross-checked against the Collatz-blind descendant arXiv:2311.17348, which proves the imaginary-quadratic analog Z(γ;α)+Z(γ;β) ≥ log log|γ|/(log log log|γ| + C). Original PDF paywalled (De Gruyter); GDZ has page images.
finds.the_match: This is the requested point-level (2,3)-rigidity to pair with the set-level Cobham note: no LARGE integer is simultaneously base-2-sparse and base-3-sparse, effectively. Instantiations: (i) Space Needle's target 2^k is maximally base-2-sparse (one nonzero digit), so any forced base-3 sparsity at hit-time would let Stewart bound all hits — GAP: nothing in the value + v2-valuation dynamics forces base-3 sparsity; (ii) the orbit prefix identity 2^k·a_k = 3^k·a_0 − Σ 3^{k−1−i}2^i e_i is a MIXED (2,3)-expansion with 0/1 digits; Stewart is about pure base-2 and base-3 expansions of the same N, so it does not directly apply to mixed expansions (that is the Newman-problem/Bajpai–Bennett world). Honest verdict: exact theorem in hand, inspiration-level match; the effective log log lower bound is the model for what a 'quantitative Cobham' should look like at points.
finds.transfer: Prove a transducer-Stewart statement: if N's base-2 expansion lies in a sparse regular language (o(log N) nonzero digits, accepted by a d-state DFA) and its base-3 expansion is also sparse-regular, then N ≤ effective bound(d) — a point-level companion to our set-level no-small-DFA-certificate theorem, with powers of 2 inside Collatz preimage languages as the first test case.

finds.ref: Csanád Bertók, Lajos Hajdu — A Hasse-type principle for exponential Diophantine equations and its applications — 2014/2016, arXiv:1407.6499, Math. Comp. 85 (2016) 849–860; plus L. Hajdu, R. Tijdeman — Skolem's conjecture confirmed for a family of exponential equations — Acta Arith. 192 (2020) 105–110
finds.the_theorem: Conjecture (their formulation of Skolem's local-global principle, verified from full text): if a_1·b_11^{α_11}···b_1ℓ^{α_1ℓ} + ... + a_k·b_k1^{α_k1}···b_kℓ^{α_kℓ} = c has NO solutions in nonnegative integers α_ij, then some modulus m ≥ 2 already obstructs it (no solutions mod m). Theorem 2.1: for fixed a_i, b_ij, the set of c violating the principle has density ZERO inside the set of non-represented c; the obstructing moduli can be forced divisible by any preassigned r; construction via Carmichael-λ-smooth moduli (generalizing Erdős–Pomerance–Schmutz). Implemented Sage solver: math.unideb.hu/~hajdul/expeqsolver.zip. Hajdu–Tijdeman 2020: Skolem's conjecture holds for u^x − v^y = 1 when one of u, v is prime (covers 2^x − 3^y = ±1) — first multi-term multiplicatively-independent family.
finds.the_match: This is the certificate theory our divergence-certificate program lives in. Solved cryptids were killed by exactly such certificates: BMO 4's residue cycle mod 3, BMO 7's parity invariant, BMO 3's invariant — worked instances of the principle. Our negative theorem (no regular invariant set of T avoiding 1 with ≤ 7 DFA states, base 2) is a lower bound in the same certificate class, since mod-2^k congruence certificates are regular: the local-global heuristic predicts certificates EXIST but our result proves they cannot be SMALL. GAPS: (a) their exponents α_ij are free; our reachability predicates have dynamics-correlated exponents — the principle formally covers only exponential-Diophantine shadows (fixed-L cycle equations, fixed-step boundary equations); (b) Theorem 2.1 is density-zero on the RIGHT-hand side c only, coefficients fixed; (c) no control of certificate size (consistent with our lower bound).
finds.transfer: Run their λ-smooth modulus-search algorithm (the actual Sage tool) on: fixed-L cycle equations n(2^K − 3^L) = Σ per residue class of n; BMO 3's 'a_n = 4^k'; Space Needle epoch equations. Outcome either way is a result: certificates found (new solved cryptids / new cycle obstructions) or systematic failure on dynamics-generated equations — evidence that cryptid predicates concentrate in the density-zero exceptional set, itself a structural theorem-shaped claim.

finds.ref: Maohua Le, Takafumi Miyazaki — Purely exponential Diophantine equations with four terms of consecutive bases: contribution to Skolem's conjecture — 2025, arXiv:2508.17601
finds.the_theorem: Theorem 1 (verified from full text): n^x = (n+1)^y + (n+2)^z + (n+3)^w has no solutions in positive integers except (n,x,y,z,w) = (2,5,1,1,2) and (3,3,2,1,1). Moreover for each n ≥ 4 they exhibit an EXPLICIT modulus M such that the equation is already insoluble mod M — verifying the Bertók–Hajdu/Skolem local-global principle for a genuinely 4-term family with multiplicatively independent bases.
finds.the_match: Four-term purely exponential equations with small bases including 2 and 3 — the same term-count regime as our shortest structures: L = 2 cycle equations n·2^K − n·3² = 3·2^{a_0} + 2^{a_1} and short Antihydra prefix identities. The paper's real value to us is the RECIPE: how proof-generated explicit moduli are constructed for 4-term equations (their M come out of the Baker-free elementary analysis). GAPS: their coefficients are all 1 and bases are consecutive n..n+3, whereas our equations carry the unbounded coefficient n and pure {2,3} bases with ordered exponents; no direct implication, only a worked template.
finds.transfer: Imitate their modulus construction for the two-parameter family n(2^K − 9) = 3·2^{a_0} + 2^{a_1} (L = 2 cycles) to produce explicit per-residue-class obstruction moduli, then compare against what our DFA search already rules out — do their moduli correspond to ≥ 8-state automata? That would locate exactly where mod-m certificates outrun small regular ones.

finds.ref: Yuri Bilu, Florian Luca, Joris Nieuwveld, Joël Ouaknine, David Purser, James Worrell — Skolem Meets Schanuel — MFCS 2022, LIPIcs 241, 20:1–20:15 (sibling: On the Skolem Problem and the Skolem Conjecture, LICS 2022)
finds.the_theorem: Verified from full text: an algorithm deciding the Skolem Problem (does an LRS have a zero term?) for SIMPLE linear recurrence sequences (simple characteristic roots). On termination it emits a stand-alone, unconditionally verifiable certificate: a partition of the sequence into finitely many subsequences, each certified either zero-free by a modulus m (subsequence nonzero mod m) or containing at most one zero via a prime p whose valuation on nonzero terms is explicitly bounded. Termination on all inputs is proved ASSUMING the Skolem Conjecture (exponential local-global principle) + the p-adic Schanuel conjecture. Implemented: skolem.mpi-sws.org.
finds.the_match: Same certificate ontology as our program, from the verification community: 'invariant partition + finite local reason for avoidance of the thin target'. Their problem (LRS hits 0 — a hyperplane) is the decidable frontier of our reachability world (generalized Collatz reachability is undecidable, Kurtz–Simon; cryptids sit between). The architecture — two-sided search: simulate forward for a zero while searching certificate space for a non-vanishing proof, with conjectures guaranteeing one side terminates — is exactly the shape a principled cryptid-decider would take. GAPS: cryptid orbits are NOT LRS (piecewise-affine with state-dependent branching); our proven-impossible certificates (≤7-state regular) are strictly richer than pure mod-m; no analog of their conditional termination theorem exists for transducer dynamics.
finds.transfer: Define Skolem-style certificates for cryptid functionals (finite automaton partitions orbit indices by parity-history; per class, a modulus or p-adic valuation certificate of boundary-avoidance) and implement the two-sided search for Antihydra/Hydra; state our ≤7-state DFA negative result as a proven lower bound on where that certificate search must begin (>7 states / moduli beyond 2^7-scale).

finds.ref: Yann Bugeaud, Pietro Corvaja, Umberto Zannier — An upper bound for the G.C.D. of a^n − 1 and b^n − 1 — 2003, Math. Z. 243, 79–84; plus Corvaja–Zannier — A lower bound for the height of a rational function at S-unit points — 2005, Monatsh. Math. (arXiv:math/0311030)
finds.the_theorem: BCZ 2003: for multiplicatively independent integers a, b ≥ 2 and any ε > 0, gcd(a^n − 1, b^n − 1) < exp(εn) for all sufficiently large n (Schmidt subspace theorem; ineffective). Generalization (verified from CZ full text): gcd(u − 1, v − 1) < max(|u|,|v|)^ε for multiplicatively independent S-units u, v ∈ Z, with finitely many exceptions; CZ 2005 extends to gcd(f(u), g(v)) for rational functions and reformulates as height lower bounds on subvarieties of G_m².
finds.the_match: The divisor-level statement that the 2-power and 3-power worlds cannot conspire: gcd(2^a − 1, 3^b − 1) is subpolynomial in the larger. Cycle relevance: a cycle demands the divisibility (2^K − 3^L) | Σ 3^{L−1−i}2^{a_i} with quotient n — a coordinated divisibility between a 2-3 power difference and a many-term {2,3}-combination, exactly the flavor the subspace-gcd method controls. GAPS, serious: (a) our pair (2^K − 3^L, Σ) is NOT of the treated separated-variable forms (u−1, v−1) or (f(u), g(v)); Σ mixes both exponent variables across many terms; (b) ineffectivity and finitely-many-exceptions block per-instance conclusions; (c) no obvious way to make n's size enter. This is a genuine new-theorem target in their framework, not an application.
finds.transfer: Target statement: gcd(2^K − 3^L, 2^a·3^b + 2^c·3^d) < max(2^K, 3^L)^ε outside explicit exceptional families. If provable by CZ height machinery on G_m^2 subvarieties, then any cycle forces n = Σ/(2^K − 3^L) to be either tiny relative to 2^K or exceptional-family — a conditional 'no large-n short cycles' theorem from pure gcd non-conspiracy.

finds.ref: A. Pethő — Perfect powers in second order linear recurrences — 1982, J. Number Theory 15, 5–13; T.N. Shorey, C.L. Stewart — ...pure powers in recurrence sequences — 1983, Math. Scand. 52, 24–36; crown: Y. Bugeaud, M. Mignotte, S. Siksek — Classical and modular approaches to exponential Diophantine equations I. Fibonacci and Lucas perfect powers — 2006, Annals of Math. 163, 969–1018
finds.the_theorem: Shorey–Stewart / Pethő (independently): a nondegenerate binary linear recurrence contains only finitely many perfect powers; the exponent q in u_n = y^q (|y| > 1) is bounded by an effectively computable constant depending only on the recurrence, and under standard conditions (dominant root; Lucas-type coprimality in Pethő) n and |y| are also effectively bounded. Higher-order: same q-bound under a dominant-root hypothesis. BMS 2006 Theorem 1 (verified from full text): the only perfect powers in the Fibonacci sequence are F_0 = 0, F_1 = F_2 = 1, F_6 = 8, F_12 = 144 (and L_1 = 1, L_3 = 4 for Lucas) — via modularity/level-lowering + linear forms in two and three logarithms.
finds.the_match: This is the industry the solved cryptid BMO 3 SHOULD have belonged to: a_0 = 2, a_n = a_{n−1} + 2^{v₂(a_{n−1})+2} − 1, halt iff a_n = 4^k ('recursive sequence hits perfect powers'). Direct answer to the brief's question (b): NO — S-unit/recurrence-power technology could not have done it off the shelf, because every hypothesis in this industry requires constant-coefficient LINEAR recurrences (or dominant roots), and BMO 3's coefficient depends on v₂ of the current state; I found no published class covering valuation-driven recurrences hitting powers (searched). The ad-hoc invariant that solved it is instead a mod-m certificate (find on Bertók–Hajdu). Residual live idea: BMO 3's v₂-self-similarity suggests (a_n) may be 2-automatic in a suitable presentation, moving the target into Adamczewski–Bell/Mahler territory (already partially in the team's collection).
finds.transfer: Two moves: (i) inventory cryptid functionals whose epoch-subsampled orbits ARE honest binary recurrences — each instantly inherits effective perfect-power finiteness (Pethő/Shorey–Stewart), giving quick wins; (ii) formalize BMO 3 as a 2-automatic sequence and ask whether 'automatic sequence ∩ perfect powers is finite' can be extracted from Mahler-method finiteness — if yes, the first non-ad-hoc proof of a solved cryptid, and a template for Space Needle's power-of-2 predicate.

best_single_bet: Bajpai–Bennett 2023 (effective 5-term S-unit equations for S = {∞,2,3}, arXiv:2308.05162). One week plays out as: Day 1-2, write the L=3-odd-step cycle equation as the 5-term S-unit equation n·2^K − n·3^3 − 9·2^{a_0} − 3·2^{a_1} − 2^{a_2} = 0 with units (2^K, 3^3·1, ...) over S = {∞,2,3}; verify Theorem 6's coefficient hypothesis H(n) ≤ κ1·h(u)^κ2 using the Rhin/two-log lower bound |2^K − 3^L| ≫ 2^K/K^{13.3} (which gives n ≤ poly(K)·3^L), and check nondegeneracy by the sign/parity argument (n odd, a_i < K kills all vanishing subsums). Day 3-4, extract the explicit effective height bound and compare with the m-cycle literature — this is the proof-of-concept that general-purpose many-term S-unit machinery reaches Collatz cycles. Day 5-7, attack the real prize: the 6-term case (L = 4) by extending their matching procedure using structure generic S-unit equations lack — monotone exponents a_0 < a_1 < ... < K and the fixed coefficient pattern (n, −n, 1, 1, 1, 1) — aiming at 'effective 6-term S-unit equations with ordered exponent structure', which would be new number theory AND a new Collatz cycle bound axis (number of TERMS = odd steps, orthogonal to the block-count axis of Simons–de Weger/Hercher).
dead_ends: (1) ||(3/2)^k|| effective lower bounds (Beukers 1981 2^{-0.9k}, Zudilin 2007 0.5803^k): already in the team's collection (zudilin-2007-lowerbound-32k.pdf); searched for post-2007 improvements — none found; and they bound frac((3/2)^k), not orbit values of floor(3n/2)-iteration, where O(1) errors accumulate — no direct Antihydra use. Do not re-search. (2) Stewart 1980 full PDF: De Gruyter paywalled; GDZ resolver serves only IIIF page images; the exact statement is safely quoted in Collatz-blind descendants (arXiv:2311.17348) — don't burn time on retrieval. The imaginary-quadratic generalization itself adds no (2,3) leverage. (3) ACM DL 403-blocks the LICS 2022 'On the Skolem Problem and the Skolem Conjecture' full text; its open MFCS 2022 sibling (downloaded) carries the same certificate architecture — use that. (4) Hunted specifically for subspace-theorem work on cycle-SHAPED equations with an UNBOUNDED free coefficient (our n) — nothing exists beyond Evertse–Győry–Stewart–Tijdeman's ineffective ω(N) ≤ 4 for N = 2^a3^b + 2^c + 3^d and Bajpai–Bennett's poly-height coefficients (Theorem 6): the unbounded-coefficient case appears to be a genuine wall in the literature, not a search miss. (5) 'Perfect powers in nonlinear/valuation-driven recurrences' (the exact BMO 3 shape): no such industry exists; every theorem requires constant-coefficient linear recurrences or dominant roots — the honest answer to whether S-unit tech could have solved BMO 3 without the ad-hoc invariant is no, only the mod-m certificate route generalizes. (6) X-coordinates-of-Pell-in-recurrences (Luca school) and Chim–Pink–Ziegler Pillai-between-recurrences: real theorems, but every hypothesis needs both sequences to be honest linear recurrences with dominant roots — cryptid orbits fail this at the same floor-function step as everything else; folded the usable part into the Bennett/Pillai find rather than a separate lead.
